stem: A patient develops widespread rash, fever, facial edema, eosinophilia, and organ involvement (hepatitis) weeks after starting a new anticonvulsant. Most likely:
rationale: DRESS = delayed onset + eosinophilia + systemic organ involvement.

stem: A patient has a full-thickness burn. The burned area is typically:
choices: Painless initially due to nerve destruction
answer: Painless initially due to nerve destruction
rationale: Full-thickness burns destroy nerve endings → can be painless.

stem: A patient develops a “target” lesion rash after a tick bite with flu-like symptoms. Most likely early diagnosis:
rationale: Erythema migrans = early Lyme presentation.

stem: Which findings raise concern for necrotizing fasciitis rather than cellulitis? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Pain out of proportion to exam
Mild localized erythema that improves with elevation immediately
answer: Pain out of proportion to exam
rationale: Rapid severe pain/systemic toxicity suggests nec fasc.

stem: A nurse suspects injury to the ulnar nerve at the elbow. Which deficit best supports this?
choices: Loss of sensation over dorsal first web space
Loss of sensation over lateral forearm
rationale: Ulnar nerve → interossei → finger ab/adduction weakness.

stem: A fracture of the proximal fibula is associated with foot drop. Which nerve is most at risk at the fibular neck?
rationale: The common fibular nerve wraps around the fibular neck before dividing.

stem: A patient has a “waiter’s tip” posture after a traction injury to the shoulder. Which nerve roots are primarily involved?
rationale: Classic Erb palsy involves C5–C6 → loss of abduction (deltoid), lateral rotation (infra/teres minor), supination (biceps).

stem: A patient has loss of hip extension strength and difficulty climbing stairs after a deep gluteal laceration. Which nerve is most likely injured?
rationale: Inferior gluteal nerve → gluteus maximus (hip extension; stairs/standing from seated).

stem: A fracture at the surgical neck of the humerus most directly endangers which structure?
choices: Radial nerve and profunda brachii artery
Axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery
Long thoracic nerve and lateral thoracic artery
answer: Axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery
rationale: Surgical neck ↔ axillary nerve + posterior circumflex humeral artery at the quadrangular space region.

stem: Scenario: A patient falls and sustains a fracture through the anatomical snuffbox region. Task: Match the injury to the most threatened structure and the most important nursing assessment focus.

middle_options: Capillary refill and pain with passive finger extension
Two-point discrimination over palmar thumb/index
Tenderness in snuffbox + follow-up for avascular necrosis risk
Sensation over dorsal ulnar hand

answer.middle: Tenderness in snuffbox + follow-up for avascular necrosis risk

rationale: Snuffbox tenderness suggests scaphoid fracture; scaphoid has retrograde blood supply, high AVN risk—nursing priority includes immobilization education and follow-up adherence.

stem: A posterior forearm laceration leads to inability to extend MCP joints, but wrist extension is partially preserved. Which nerve branch is most likely injured?
rationale: Posterior interosseous nerve (deep radial) supplies finger extensors; superficial radial is sensory.

stem: A patient cannot extend the knee against resistance after a femoral nerve block. Which muscle group is primarily affected?
rationale: Femoral nerve → quadriceps (knee extension) + anterior thigh sensation.

stem: A nurse suspects an Achilles tendon rupture. Which movement will be most impaired?
rationale: Achilles transmits gastrocnemius/soleus force → plantarflexion.

stem: A patient cannot flex the DIP joint of the index finger after a forearm laceration; thumb opposition is also weak. Which nerve is most likely injured?
choices: Median nerve at the carpal tunnel
Radial nerve in the radial groove
rationale: Anterior interosseous nerve (AIN) (motor branch of median) → FDP (index), FPL, pronator quadratus; classic “OK sign” weakness.

stem: A nurse palpates the posterior tibial pulse. Where is the best landmark?
choices: Dorsum of foot between first and second metatarsals
rationale: Posterior tibial artery runs posterior to medial malleolus.

stem: A nurse is teaching safe IM injection into the deltoid. To reduce risk of axillary nerve injury, the injection should be placed:
choices: 2–3 fingerbreadths below the acromion, in the thickest deltoid mass
At the surgical neck of the humerus level
Near the posterior border of the deltoid
answer: 2–3 fingerbreadths below the acromion, in the thickest deltoid mass
rationale: Axillary nerve courses near the surgical neck; safe deltoid IM is 2–3 fingerbreadths below acromion.

stem: A patient with a pelvic fracture has inability to adduct the thigh and medial thigh numbness. Which nerve is compromised?
rationale: Obturator nerve → adductors + medial thigh sensation.

stem: A long-line IV attempt accidentally cannulates the basilic vein and causes a deep hematoma in the medial arm. Which nerve is most likely compressed as swelling spreads in the medial bicipital groove?
rationale: The ulnar nerve courses medially and posterior to the medial epicondyle; medial arm swelling can track and compress it (clinical neuro check: ring/small finger sensation + intrinsic hand strength).

stem: Which muscles form the rotator cuff? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: SITS = supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis.

stem: Which structures pass through the inguinal canal? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Inguinal canal contents include spermatic cord/round ligament + ilioinguinal nerve + genital branch of genitofemoral. Femoral nerve is under inguinal ligament; external iliac artery becomes femoral under ligament.

stem: A nurse palpates the femoral pulse at the midinguinal point. That point is best described as the midpoint between:
rationale: Femoral artery at midinguinal point = midpoint between ASIS and pubic symphysis?

stem: A patient with carpal tunnel syndrome has thenar weakness and paresthesia in the lateral 3½ digits. Which sensory region is often spared, helping differentiate from a more proximal median neuropathy?
choices: Palmar aspect of the thenar eminence
Palmar aspect of the index finger
Dorsal aspect of the distal phalanx of the middle finger
answer: Palmar aspect of the thenar eminence
rationale: Palmar cutaneous branch of median nerve arises proximal to carpal tunnel → thenar eminence skin spared.

stem: A midshaft humeral fracture is suspected. Which findings most specifically support injury to the radial nerve in the radial groove? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Loss of sensation in dorsal first web space
answer: Loss of sensation in dorsal first web space
rationale: Radial nerve → wrist/finger extensors + sensation in dorsal first web space. Triceps (elbow extension) may be spared depending on lesion level; thumb opposition = median; finger abduction = ulnar.

stem: A patient has a posterior hip dislocation after an MVC. Which structure is most immediately at risk from the dislocation itself?
rationale: Posterior dislocation commonly endangers the sciatic nerve as the femoral neurovascular bundle is anterior.

stem: Which structures are components of a typical thoracic vertebra? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Thoracic vertebrae have costal facets, long down-slanting spinous processes, and relatively heart-shaped bodies. Transverse foramina = cervical; bifid spinous = typical cervical.

stem: During a rapid trauma assessment, a nurse notes loss of sensation over the “regimental badge” area with weak shoulder abduction. Which nerve lesion is most consistent?
rationale: Axillary nerve → deltoid/teres minor + sensory patch over lateral shoulder (“regimental badge”).

stem: A lumbar disc herniation compresses the L5 nerve root. Which motor deficit is most consistent?
choices: Loss of ankle dorsiflexion (foot drop tendency)
answer: Loss of ankle dorsiflexion (foot drop tendency)
rationale: L5 contributes strongly to dorsiflexion (tibialis anterior via deep fibular) and great toe extension.

stem: Scenario: A patient with an ankle fracture has numbness on the plantar surface of the foot and weakness of toe flexion after swelling worsens near the medial malleolus. Task: Choose the most likely entrapment site and the most affected nerve.

middle_options: Tibial nerve
Common fibular nerve
Deep fibular nerve
Saphenous nerve

answer.middle: Tibial nerve

rationale: Tarsal tunnel (posterior to medial malleolus) compresses tibial nerve → plantar sensory loss + intrinsic foot weakness/toe flexion issues.

stem: Which structures pass through the greater sciatic foramen? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Many structures exit pelvis via greater sciatic foramen; pudendal nerve exits (then re-enters via lesser sciatic foramen). Femoral nerve passes under inguinal ligament.

stem: A pelvic fracture damages the pudendal nerve in Alcock canal. Which deficit is most expected?
choices: Loss of perineal sensation and external anal sphincter weakness
answer: Loss of perineal sensation and external anal sphincter weakness
rationale: Pudendal nerve → perineal sensation + external anal sphincter and urethral sphincter control (nursing: continence assessment).

stem: A patient has loss of knee extension and diminished patellar reflex. Which spinal nerve roots are most associated with the patellar reflex?
rationale: Patellar reflex is primarily L3–L4 via femoral nerve.

stem: After a neck stab wound, the patient has loss of scapular retraction strength but shoulder shrug is intact. Which nerve is most likely injured?
rationale: Dorsal scapular nerve → rhomboids (retraction) + levator scapulae; shrug (trapezius) is CN XI.

stem: A patient has a winged scapula with preserved shoulder shrug and normal arm sensation. Which movement will be most impaired?
choices: Protracting the scapula and upwardly rotating it for overhead reach
answer: Protracting the scapula and upwardly rotating it for overhead reach
rationale: Serratus anterior (long thoracic) protracts + upwardly rotates scapula; required for overhead elevation.

stem: Which statements about the femoral triangle are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Contains femoral nerve, artery, and vein
Femoral nerve is lateral to the femoral artery
Femoral vein is medial to the femoral artery
Femoral canal contains the femoral nerve
The floor includes iliopsoas and pectineus
answer: Contains femoral nerve, artery, and vein
Femoral nerve is lateral to the femoral artery
Femoral vein is medial to the femoral artery
The floor includes iliopsoas and pectineus
rationale: NAVEL (lateral→medial): Nerve, Artery, Vein, Empty space (canal), Lymph.

stem: Scenario: After a tibial plateau fracture, the patient has escalating pain, tense swelling, and pain with passive toe extension. Task: Select the most likely compartment and the most endangered neurovascular structure.

middle_options: Tibial nerve + posterior tibial artery
Deep fibular nerve + anterior tibial artery
Superficial fibular nerve + fibular artery
Saphenous nerve + femoral artery

answer.middle: Deep fibular nerve + anterior tibial artery

rationale: Pain with passive toe extension + tense anterior leg suggests anterior compartment syndrome → threatens deep fibular nerve and anterior tibial artery; urgent escalation.

stem: Which structures pass through the carpal tunnel? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Carpal tunnel = median nerve, FDS, FDP, FPL. Ulnar nerve passes through Guyon canal. FCR tendon runs in its own compartment/lateral groove.

stem: A patient has a slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE). Which vascular concern makes prompt management important?
choices: Disruption of retinacular vessels to the femoral head
answer: Disruption of retinacular vessels to the femoral head
rationale: SCFE can compromise vessels supplying femoral head → AVN risk.

stem: Which tendons are found in the first dorsal extensor compartment of the wrist? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: 1st compartment = APL + EPB (De Quervain).

stem: A patient has numbness over the lateral forearm with weakness in elbow flexion, but hand sensation is intact. Which nerve is most likely injured?
rationale: Musculocutaneous → biceps/brachialis (elbow flexion) + lateral forearm cutaneous (via lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve).

stem: Scenario: A patient has a deep laceration just proximal to the flexor retinaculum with loss of thumb opposition and sensory loss in lateral 3½ digits, but sensation over thenar eminence is intact. Task: Choose the most likely lesion location and the key reasoning clue.

left_options: Median nerve in the forearm proximal to palmar cutaneous branch
Radial nerve superficial branch at wrist
middle_options: Palmar cutaneous branch spared → thenar eminence sensation preserved
Dorsal hand sensation spared → points to ulnar lesion
Wrist drop → points to radial groove lesion
Loss of finger abduction → points to interossei paralysis

answer.middle: Palmar cutaneous branch spared → thenar eminence sensation preserved

rationale: Thenar eminence skin is supplied by palmar cutaneous branch (arises proximal to tunnel), so carpal tunnel fits best.

stem: Scenario: A casted forearm patient reports escalating pain unrelieved by opioids, pain with passive finger extension, and paresthesia. Task: Choose the most likely compartment syndrome location and the most endangered nerve.

middle_options: Median nerve
Radial nerve
Ulnar nerve
Musculocutaneous nerve

answer.middle: Median nerve

rationale: Forearm compartment syndrome commonly threatens median nerve in the flexor compartment; pain with passive extension is a hallmark. Nursing priority: notify provider immediately, keep limb at heart level, frequent neurovascular checks.

stem: A patient has Trendelenburg gait (pelvis drops on the contralateral side when standing on the affected limb). Which nerve is injured?
rationale: Superior gluteal nerve → gluteus medius/minimus weakness → contralateral pelvic drop.

stem: A patient with an intertrochanteric femur fracture has intact femoral head perfusion but severe pain. Why is the femoral head less threatened by AVN than with a femoral neck fracture?
choices: Retinacular vessels are less likely disrupted
Femoral nerve maintains blood flow via vasa nervorum
Obturator artery supplies the head directly in adults
answer: Retinacular vessels are less likely disrupted
rationale: Femoral neck fractures disrupt retinacular branches; intertrochanteric fractures are extracapsular → retinacular vessels more preserved.

stem: A patient cannot dorsiflex the foot and has numbness in the first dorsal web space. Which injury patterns could cause this? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Tibial nerve compression in tarsal tunnel
rationale: First web space + dorsiflexion = deep fibular nerve (or L5). Fibular neck hits common fibular nerve; anterior compartment syndrome hits deep fibular; tarsal tunnel = tibial nerve; posterior hip dislocation = sciatic (can affect fibular division but classic sign here points to deep fibular/L5).

stem: A patient cannot initiate shoulder abduction from 0–15° but can abduct beyond that with assistance. Which muscle is primarily weak?
rationale: Supraspinatus initiates first ~15° of abduction; deltoid dominates after.

stem: A nurse is assessing suspected rib fracture pain and wants to understand why deep inspiration is limited. Which muscle is the primary inspiratory muscle that increases thoracic volume by lowering the diaphragm?
rationale: Diaphragm is primary inspiratory muscle.

stem: A nurse assesses a patient with scapular winging that worsens when pushing against a wall. Which nerve is injured?
rationale: Long thoracic nerve → serratus anterior paralysis → medial scapular winging (critical for safe shoulder mechanics).

stem: A fracture at the medial epicondyle of the humerus most directly risks which deficit?
rationale: Medial epicondyle (“funny bone” region) → ulnar nerve → interossei weakness (finger ab/adduction).

stem: A nurse suspects compartment syndrome in the leg. Which compartment contains the deep fibular (peroneal) nerve and the anterior tibial artery?
rationale: Anterior compartment = deep fibular nerve + anterior tibial vessels; classic “pain with passive toe extension,” foot drop risk.

stem: A patient with a fractured clavicle has drooping shoulder and difficulty shrugging, but arm abduction is mostly intact. Which nerve is most likely injured?
rationale: Shrug = trapezius → spinal accessory nerve (CN XI).

stem: A nurse is concerned about avascular necrosis after a femoral neck fracture. Which arterial supply is most critical to the femoral head in adults?
choices: Retinacular branches of the medial circumflex femoral artery
answer: Retinacular branches of the medial circumflex femoral artery
rationale: Adult femoral head mainly from retinacular branches (medial circumflex femoral). Ligamentum teres artery is usually insufficient in adults.

stem: A nurse palpates the “safe IM injection site” in the ventrogluteal region. The injection is intended to avoid the sciatic nerve. Which muscle is the primary target?
rationale: Ventrogluteal IM targets gluteus medius (and minimus), away from sciatic nerve pathways.

stem: In an anterior shoulder dislocation, which muscle is most commonly torn because it resists anterior translation?
rationale: Subscapularis is the primary anterior rotator cuff stabilizer.

stem: Which findings are most consistent with anterior interosseous nerve (AIN) palsy? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Inability to make an “OK” sign (weak FPL and FDP to index)
Loss of sensation over palmar thumb
answer: Inability to make an “OK” sign (weak FPL and FDP to index)
rationale: AIN is motor only to FPL, FDP (index/middle), pronator quadratus.

stem: A patient has loss of pain/temperature on the left beginning ~2 segments below a right-sided cord lesion, with right-sided weakness and right-sided loss of vibration/proprioception below the lesion. Diagnosis:
rationale: Hemicord lesion = ipsi motor + ipsi DCML, contra spinothalamic (often a few levels below).

stem: A patient has a right eye that cannot look down and in, with vertical diplopia that worsens when walking downstairs. Which nerve is injured?
rationale: CN IV innervates superior oblique (down/in), worsens with stairs/reading.

stem: A patient has loss of pain/temperature sensation on the right side of the face only, with intact corneal reflex and normal facial motor function. The lesion is most likely in:
rationale: Facial pain/temperature mediated via spinal trigeminal nucleus/tract; corneal reflex afferent is V1 (can be preserved if partial/limited distribution).

stem: A patient has a lesion affecting the posterior limb of the internal capsule. Which deficit is most expected?
rationale: Posterior limb carries corticospinal fibers → contralateral pure motor stroke.

stem: A patient with a pituitary macroadenoma develops loss of peripheral vision in both eyes. Which structure is compressed?
rationale: Pituitary mass compresses optic chiasm → bitemporal hemianopsia.

stem: Scenario: A patient develops diplopia, ptosis, and a fixed dilated pupil after head trauma. Task: Select the most likely nerve and the highest-priority physiologic concern.

middle_options: Uncal herniation compressing CN III
Lateral medullary infarct affecting nucleus ambiguus
Cavernous sinus thrombosis affecting CN VII
Cerebellar stroke causing intention tremor

answer.middle: Uncal herniation compressing CN III

rationale: CN III palsy with blown pupil post-trauma is a red flag for uncal herniation (ICP emergency).

stem: A patient has a lesion of the substantia nigra pars compacta. Which neurotransmitter deficit is primary?
rationale: Substantia nigra → dopamine to striatum; loss → Parkinsonian features.

stem: A patient has ipsilateral loss of vibration/proprioception below the lesion and contralateral loss of pain/temperature starting a few levels below after a penetrating hemicord injury. This is classic for:
rationale: Hemicord = Brown-Séquard (ipsi dorsal column + motor; contra pain/temp).

stem: A patient with a suspected brainstem stroke has impaired adduction of the right eye on left gaze with abducting nystagmus of the left eye. Convergence is intact. The lesion is in the:
rationale: INO = MLF lesion; convergence often preserved.

stem: A patient has pure motor hemiparesis of face/arm/leg with no cortical signs (no aphasia, no neglect). Most likely lesion:
rationale: Internal capsule lacunar infarct produces pure motor deficits.

stem: A patient has a lesion below the pyramidal decussation. Which motor deficit pattern is expected?
choices: No weakness because corticospinal fibers are uncrossed
rationale: Below decussation (spinal cord): corticospinal tract already crossed → ipsilateral motor deficit.

stem: A nurse identifies a patient with loss of knee extension and absent patellar reflex (L3–L4) plus anterior thigh sensory loss. The lesion most likely involves:
rationale: Femoral nerve = quadriceps + patellar reflex + anterior thigh sensation.

stem: A patient with multiple sclerosis has internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO). The damaged tract is the:
rationale: INO = lesion of MLF, coordinating CN VI and CN III nuclei for horizontal gaze.

stem: A nurse is assessing for early signs of increased ICP. Which finding is typically earliest?
choices: Subtle change in level of consciousness
answer: Subtle change in level of consciousness
rationale: Earliest sign is often LOC change (restlessness, confusion) before late brainstem signs.

stem: Which spinal cord tracts are primarily involved in fine touch, vibration, and proprioception? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Dorsal column–medial lemniscus pathway carries vibration/proprioception. Spinocerebellar is unconscious proprioception.

stem: Which findings suggest lower motor neuron facial nerve palsy rather than an upper motor neuron lesion? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Inability to close the eye on affected side
Contralateral lower facial droop only with forehead sparing
answer: Inability to close the eye on affected side
rationale: LMN CN VII affects entire ipsilateral face and can involve stapedius (hyperacusis). UMN spares forehead. Gag reflex is CN IX/X.

stem: A patient has urinary retention after spinal anesthesia. The most direct autonomic mechanism is blockade of:
choices: Cranial nerve X parasympathetics to pelvic organs
rationale: Detrusor contraction is parasympathetic S2–S4; blockade → urinary retention.

stem: A patient has aphasia and right-sided weakness, with gaze deviation toward the left hemisphere lesion. Most likely vascular territory:
rationale: Dominant hemisphere (usually left) MCA → aphasia + contralateral face/arm weakness and gaze preference.

stem: A nurse is localizing a suspected lateral medullary (Wallenberg) stroke. Which findings support this diagnosis? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Lateral medulla hits spinal trigeminal nucleus (ipsi face P/T), spinothalamic tract (contra body P/T), nucleus ambiguus (CN IX/X → dysphagia/hoarseness). Tongue deviation = medial medulla (CN XII). Vibration/proprioception = medial lemniscus (medial).

stem: A patient has ipsilateral facial paralysis (including forehead), hyperacusis, loss of taste on the anterior 2/3 of the tongue, and decreased lacrimation after a temporal bone fracture. The lesion is most consistent with injury to CN VII at:
choices: Internal acoustic meatus / facial canal proximal to chorda tympani and greater petrosal
Cerebellopontine angle affecting only motor root
answer: Internal acoustic meatus / facial canal proximal to chorda tympani and greater petrosal
rationale: Proximal CN VII lesion before branches → facial paralysis + taste + lacrimation + stapedius (hyperacusis). Stylomastoid/parotid lesions spare taste/lacrimation.

stem: A nurse is screening for cerebellar dysfunction. Which findings are cerebellar in origin? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Cerebellar = coordination/ataxia. Rest tremor = basal ganglia; spasticity/hyperreflexia = UMN.

stem: A comatose patient has unilateral fixed and dilated pupil with contralateral weakness. The most urgent anatomical process is:
choices: Uncal herniation compressing ipsilateral CN III
Herniation compressing CN VI in cavernous sinus
answer: Uncal herniation compressing ipsilateral CN III
rationale: Unilateral blown pupil = CN III compression (uncal herniation) → emergent.

stem: A patient has loss of ipsilateral facial motor function, decreased lacrimation/salivation, and loss of taste anterior 2/3 tongue plus vertigo. Most likely vascular territory:
rationale: AICA lateral pontine syndrome involves facial nucleus/CN VII and vestibular structures.

stem: Scenario: After epidural anesthesia, a postpartum patient develops severe headache that worsens when upright and improves when supine. Task: Choose the anatomic cause and the best nursing teaching priority.

left_options: CSF leak from dural puncture (low CSF pressure)
middle_options: Report worsening headache, neck stiffness, neuro changes; consider epidural blood patch discussion
Avoid fluids; dehydration reduces headache
Encourage rapid ambulation to normalize CSF pressure
This is expected and requires no follow-up

answer.left: CSF leak from dural puncture (low CSF pressure)
answer.middle: Report worsening headache, neck stiffness, neuro changes; consider epidural blood patch discussion

rationale: Post–dural puncture headache is positional from CSF leak; nursing includes monitoring, hydration/caffeine per protocol, escalation, and education (blood patch option).

stem: Which features best characterize subarachnoid hemorrhage from a ruptured berry aneurysm? (Select all that apply.)
choices: CSF with elevated RBCs that do not clear
Unilateral facial paralysis with forehead sparing
answer: CSF with elevated RBCs that do not clear
rationale: Classic SAH findings. Forehead sparing facial weakness = UMN facial palsy.

stem: A patient has right tongue deviation, contralateral body weakness, and contralateral loss of vibration/proprioception after stroke. This is most consistent with:
rationale: Medial medulla affects CN XII (tongue), pyramids (motor), medial lemniscus (DCML).

stem: A patient has contralateral loss of pain/temperature from the body with ipsilateral loss of facial pain/temperature, plus ipsilateral ataxia and Horner syndrome. The best single localization is:
rationale: Crossed facial/body pain-temp + Horner + ataxia strongly indicates lateral brainstem (PICA/AICA region).

stem: A patient has contralateral loss of vibration/proprioception and contralateral weakness after a lesion above the pyramidal decussation. Where is the most likely lesion?
rationale: Above decussation: corticospinal and dorsal column pathways already crossed (motor crossed at pyramids; DCML crossed in medulla) → contralateral deficits.

stem: A patient has LMN signs at the level of injury but UMN signs below (after recovery from spinal shock). This pattern best describes:
rationale: Cord lesions create segmental LMN damage with descending tract UMN signs below.

stem: Which cranial nerves carry parasympathetic fibers? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Parasympathetics via CN III, VII, IX, X.

stem: Scenario: A patient with suspected meningitis needs an explanation of why neck stiffness occurs. Task: Choose the meningeal layer involved in pain sensitivity and the clinical implication for nursing care.

middle_options: Dura irritation causes pain; minimize stimuli and monitor for ICP changes
Pia irritation causes dermatomal pain; assess for foot drop
Arachnoid irritation causes focal motor deficits only; assess for Babinski
Ependyma irritation causes cranial nerve VII palsy; assess lacrimation

answer.middle: Dura irritation causes pain; minimize stimuli and monitor for ICP changes

rationale: Dura is pain-sensitive (meningeal irritation → headache/neck stiffness). Nursing focus includes neuro monitoring and managing noxious stimuli while escalating concerns.

stem: A nurse suspects Horner syndrome. Which findings are expected? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Sympathetic disruption → ptosis (Müller muscle), miosis, anhidrosis. “Down and out” = CN III palsy.

stem: A patient has inability to abduct either eye during horizontal gaze to the right (both eyes fail to look right). This localizes best to:
rationale: Abducens nucleus coordinates ipsilateral lateral rectus + contralateral medial rectus via MLF; lesion causes ipsilateral gaze palsy.

stem: Which findings suggest a lateral pontine (AICA) syndrome rather than a lateral medullary (PICA) syndrome? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: AICA can involve facial nucleus/CN VII → facial paralysis + lacrimation/salivation deficits; both can have contralateral body P/T loss. Dysphagia/hoarseness = nucleus ambiguus (medulla/PICA). Facial P/T loss can occur in both; the differentiator is CN VII LMN signs.

stem: A patient has loss of pain/temperature in a “cape-like” distribution over the shoulders and upper limbs. The most likely lesion is:
choices: Anterior cord syndrome involving corticospinal tract
rationale: Syrinx disrupts decussating spinothalamic fibers at anterior white commissure.

stem: Scenario: A patient develops sudden severe headache, photophobia, nuchal rigidity, and vomiting. Task: Choose the most likely bleeding location and the best immediate nursing priority focus.

middle_options: Airway/LOC monitoring and rapid escalation; anticipate vasospasm monitoring protocols
Immediate leg elevation and compression stockings
Encourage ambulation to reduce clot risk
Start oral antibiotics only and discharge if improved

answer.middle: Airway/LOC monitoring and rapid escalation; anticipate vasospasm monitoring protocols

rationale: SAH causes meningeal irritation; nursing priorities are neuro monitoring, airway/LOC, rapid escalation, and anticipating complications like vasospasm.

stem: Scenario: A patient presents with vertigo, nausea, ipsilateral facial paralysis, decreased lacrimation, and hearing loss. Task: Choose the vascular territory and the key structure explaining hearing loss.

middle_options: Cochlear nucleus / CN VIII involvement
Nucleus ambiguus involvement
Hypoglossal nucleus involvement
Medial lemniscus involvement

answer.middle: Cochlear nucleus / CN VIII involvement

rationale: AICA lateral pontine syndrome can include CN VII + CN VIII structures → facial paralysis + hearing loss.

stem: A patient has a right CN III palsy (ptosis, “down and out,” blown pupil) and left-sided weakness. This pattern most strongly localizes to:
rationale: CN III fascicles + corticospinal tract involvement = midbrain (e.g., Weber syndrome).

stem: A patient has loss of pain and temperature with preservation of light touch after injury. Which pathway most directly mediates pain/temperature?
rationale: Pain/temperature → spinothalamic.

stem: Which structures are typically found within the cavernous sinus? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Cavernous sinus contains ICA and CN III, IV, V1, V2, VI. CN VII is not in cavernous sinus.

stem: A patient has loss of pain/temperature on the left body and right face after stroke. This “crossed” sensory pattern most strongly suggests a lesion in the:
rationale: Crossed findings (face vs body) point to brainstem where trigeminal and spinothalamic pathways are close.

stem: A patient has anisocoria that worsens in bright light (one pupil fails to constrict). This most strongly suggests dysfunction of:
choices: Optic nerve (CN II) afferent limb only
rationale: In bright light, the abnormal pupil is the one that cannot constrict → parasympathetic (CN III).

stem: A patient has diplopia and inability to abduct the right eye. Which cranial nerve is involved?
rationale: Abduction = lateral rectus = CN VI.

stem: A patient has weakness and atrophy of the tongue on the right with deviation to the right on protrusion. The lesion is:
choices: Right corticobulbar fibers to CN XII
Left corticobulbar fibers to CN XII
rationale: LMN hypoglossal lesion → tongue deviates toward lesion with atrophy/fasciculations.

stem: A patient has left facial pain/temperature loss, right body pain/temperature loss, hoarseness, and vertigo. The lesion is most consistent with an infarct of the:
rationale: Classic lateral medulla (Wallenberg) = crossed P/T, nucleus ambiguus (hoarseness), vestibular symptoms.

stem: A patient has facial droop, decreased lacrimation, decreased salivation, and loss of taste from anterior 2/3 tongue. Which cranial nerve is involved?
rationale: CN VII carries taste (chorda tympani) + parasympathetics to lacrimal/submandibular/sublingual glands.

stem: A patient has severe dysphagia and absent gag reflex after a stroke. Which nucleus is primarily involved?
rationale: Nucleus ambiguus contains motor fibers of CN IX/X → swallowing/phonation.

stem: A comatose patient has pupils that are equal, pinpoint, and reactive to light. Which neuroanatomic region is most implicated?
rationale: Pontine lesions can cause pinpoint reactive pupils (sympathetic pathway disruption with intact parasympathetics).

stem: A nurse notes that the corneal reflex is absent in the right eye when the right cornea is touched, but the patient can still blink both eyes when the left cornea is touched. This pattern suggests damage to:
rationale: Corneal reflex afferent = V1. If touching right cornea produces no blink (both sides), afferent on right is impaired; intact response when left is touched indicates efferent (VII) intact.

stem: A nurse suspects cauda equina syndrome rather than conus medullaris. Which findings are more consistent with cauda equina? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Cauda equina = peripheral nerve roots → asymmetric LMN deficits + radicular pain. Conus often has earlier symmetric bladder/bowel issues.

stem: A patient’s voice is hoarse, and laryngoscopy shows the right vocal fold cannot abduct (opens poorly during inspiration). The most likely paralyzed muscle is:
rationale: Posterior cricoarytenoid is the only vocal fold abductor.

stem: A patient with COPD has chronically elevated CO₂. The primary compensatory mechanism over days is:
rationale: Chronic respiratory acidosis → kidneys retain HCO₃⁻.

stem: A patient has a chylothorax. The injured structure most likely is:
rationale: Chyle leak typically from thoracic duct.

stem: A patient develops acute stridor after extubation. The most likely anatomic cause is:
choices: Upper airway narrowing at larynx (edema/vocal cords)
answer: Upper airway narrowing at larynx (edema/vocal cords)
rationale: Stridor = upper airway obstruction, commonly laryngeal edema.

stem: A tall, thin young adult develops a spontaneous pneumothorax. The most likely site of bleb rupture is:
rationale: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax often from apical subpleural blebs.

stem: Scenario: A patient with COPD becomes somnolent with shallow respirations. ABG suggests severe hypercapnia. Task: Choose the most likely anatomic/physiologic driver and the priority nursing action.

left_options: Loss of alveolar surface area only with normal ventilation
Hypoventilation with impaired CO₂ elimination (alveolar ventilation failure)
Pure diffusion limitation without ventilation issues
middle_options: Support ventilation (e.g., NIV/BiPAP or prepare for airway support per protocol) and monitor LOC
Restrict oxygen completely
Give diuretics as definitive immediate treatment
Encourage ambulation and fluids only

answer.left: Hypoventilation with impaired CO₂ elimination (alveolar ventilation failure)
answer.middle: Support ventilation (e.g., NIV/BiPAP or prepare for airway support per protocol) and monitor LOC

rationale: CO₂ retention reflects ventilatory failure; nursing priority is ventilatory support and airway readiness.

stem: A patient has loss of high-pitched phonation (difficulty increasing pitch) but otherwise normal vocal fold movement. Most likely nerve injured:
choices: External branch of superior laryngeal nerve
Internal branch of superior laryngeal nerve
answer: External branch of superior laryngeal nerve
rationale: External superior laryngeal → cricothyroid (tenses cords for pitch).

stem: A patient has pleural effusion. On upright chest x-ray, fluid accumulates most in the:
rationale: Pleural fluid layers dependently → costodiaphragmatic recess.

stem: The phrenic nerve supplies motor innervation to the diaphragm and arises from spinal roots:
A foreign body lodges in the right lower lobe bronchus. Which bronchopulmonary segment is most commonly involved in supine aspiration?
choices: Superior segment of right lower lobe
Medial basal segment of right lower lobe
Anterior segment of right upper lobe only
Lingular segment of left upper lobe
answer: Superior segment of right lower lobe
rationale: Supine aspiration often goes to superior segment of lower lobes (especially right).

stem: A thoracentesis for pleural effusion is typically performed in the midaxillary line, inserting the needle just superior to a rib, usually around:
rationale: Effusion collects in dependent pleural recess; common site is 7th–9th ICS MAL (avoid going too low).

stem: A patient has severe asthma. The main site of increased airway resistance is typically:
choices: Small and medium bronchi/bronchioles due to smooth muscle constriction
answer: Small and medium bronchi/bronchioles due to smooth muscle constriction
rationale: Asthma increases resistance in bronchi/bronchioles.

stem: A patient has decreased diaphragmatic movement on the right due to phrenic nerve injury. The spinal roots of the phrenic nerve are:
rationale: Phrenic = C3–C5 (“keeps the diaphragm alive”).

stem: A chest tube is placed in the “safe triangle.” The recommended general intercostal space and line for tube thoracostomy is near:
rationale: Safe triangle area commonly around 4th–5th ICS anterior/mid-axillary region.

stem: Which structures are part of the upper airway? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Upper airway: nose → pharynx → larynx. Trachea onward is lower.

stem: Which findings are most consistent with tension pneumothorax? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Tracheal deviation away from affected side (late)
Bilateral crackles as the primary initial finding
answer: Tracheal deviation away from affected side (late)
rationale: Tension PTX causes obstructive shock and unilateral findings.

stem: A patient has lobar pneumonia in the right middle lobe. Which auscultation area best corresponds?
choices: Right upper anterior chest near clavicle
rationale: Right middle lobe is more anterior/lateral; posterior exam may miss it.

stem: A patient has a pulmonary infarct after PE. Which feature of the lung makes infarction less common than in other organs?
rationale: Dual supply reduces infarct risk (though pleural-based infarcts can occur).

stem: Which statements about the right main bronchus compared with the left are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: More likely to receive aspirated foreign bodies
Gives rise to fewer lobar bronchi
answer: More likely to receive aspirated foreign bodies
rationale: Right main bronchus is wider and more vertical, aspiration risk higher. Left is longer; right has 3 lobar bronchi.

stem: A patient aspirates while supine. Which lung region is most likely to receive aspirated material?
choices: Right upper lobe anterior segment only
rationale: Right main bronchus is more vertical; in supine aspiration tends to the posterior segments of the upper lobes and superior/posterior segments of lower lobes, especially on the right—classically right lower lobe posterior segments.

stem: Which findings are consistent with pleural effusion on physical exam? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Effusion dampens transmission → dullness + decreased breath sounds/fremitus.

stem: A patient has a widened mediastinum after trauma. The most concerning vascular injury is:
rationale: Widened mediastinum in trauma → suspect aortic injury.

stem: Which structure is most likely to be injured by a posterior basal segmentectomy of the right lower lobe if dissection strays intersegmentally?
rationale: Pulmonary veins run between segments and are at risk in intersegmental planes.

stem: Scenario: After neck surgery, a patient has a weak cough, hoarse voice, and aspiration with liquids. Laryngoscopy shows one vocal fold immobilized near midline. Task: Choose the most likely nerve injured and the most immediate nursing priority.

middle_options: Protect airway: strict swallow precautions, aspiration precautions, and rapid provider/SLP evaluation
Encourage thin liquids to “practice swallowing”
Place patient flat and give large oral fluids
Ignore aspiration risk if oxygen saturation is normal

answer.middle: Protect airway: strict swallow precautions, aspiration precautions, and rapid provider/SLP evaluation

rationale: RLN injury → vocal fold immobility + aspiration risk; nursing priority is airway protection.

stem: Which statements about the recurrent laryngeal nerves are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Right recurrent laryngeal loops under the right subclavian artery
Left recurrent laryngeal loops under the aortic arch near ligamentum arteriosum
Both are branches of CN X
Both run anterior to the trachea throughout their course
Injury can cause hoarseness and aspiration risk
answer: Right recurrent laryngeal loops under the right subclavian artery
Left recurrent laryngeal loops under the aortic arch near ligamentum arteriosum
Both are branches of CN X
Injury can cause hoarseness and aspiration risk
rationale: They ascend in the tracheoesophageal groove; not anterior throughout.

stem: In an upright adult, the inferior border of the lung at the midclavicular line is approximately at:
rationale: Lung: MCL ~ 6th rib; pleura extends lower.

stem: A child with epiglottitis is drooling, tripod positioning, and has muffled “hot potato” voice. The priority anatomical concern is:
rationale: Epiglottic swelling can quickly obstruct the supraglottic airway.

stem: A patient has hoarseness and left vocal cord paralysis from lung cancer. The most likely site of tumor is near:
rationale: Left recurrent laryngeal nerve loops under aortic arch—mediastinal tumors can compress it.

stem: In an upright person, which lung region has the highest V/Q ratio?
choices: Depends only on right vs left lung
rationale: Apex has relatively lower perfusion than ventilation → higher V/Q.

stem: Which anatomic features increase airway resistance in asthma? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Asthma: bronchoconstriction + edema + mucus → increased resistance.

stem: Scenario: A patient with chest trauma is hypotensive with absent breath sounds on the right and tracheal deviation to the left. Task: Choose the diagnosis and the immediate intervention.

middle_options: Needle decompression then chest tube
IV antibiotics
Diuretics and nitrates
Incentive spirometry only

answer.middle: Needle decompression then chest tube

rationale: Tension pneumothorax is obstructive shock → decompress immediately.

stem: Which pleural layers/regions are innervated by the phrenic nerve and can refer pain to the shoulder? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Phrenic supplies fibrous pericardium and parietal pleura (mediastinal + central diaphragmatic). Costal pleura via intercostal nerves; visceral pleura is insensitive.

stem: A patient has decreased breath sounds and hyperresonance on the right; trachea deviates left. The immediate life-threatening diagnosis is:
rationale: Tension PTX causes mediastinal shift and shock risk.

stem: Which are “don’t miss” causes of sudden respiratory compromise that are primarily anatomic obstruction/pressure problems? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: PTX (pressure), foreign body (obstruction), PE (vascular obstruction) are immediate life threats.

stem: A patient has profound hypoxemia that improves minimally with high-flow oxygen after lobar pneumonia. The primary physiology is most consistent with:
rationale: Consolidated alveoli are perfused but not ventilated → shunt; oxygen has limited effect.

stem: Scenario: A ventilated patient with status asthmaticus becomes hypotensive; you note rising peak pressures and incomplete exhalation. Task: Choose the dominant problem and the best immediate action.

left_options: Auto-PEEP/dynamic hyperinflation causing decreased venous return
middle_options: Temporarily disconnect from ventilator to allow full exhalation (per protocol) and adjust settings to increase expiratory time
Increase respiratory rate to normalize PaCO₂ quickly
Increase tidal volume to “open” airways
Give diuretics first-line

answer.left: Auto-PEEP/dynamic hyperinflation causing decreased venous return
answer.middle: Temporarily disconnect from ventilator to allow full exhalation (per protocol) and adjust settings to increase expiratory time

rationale: Air trapping causes auto-PEEP and shock physiology; immediate relief is allowing exhalation and ventilator adjustments + bronchodilation.

stem: Scenario: A patient has acute hypoxemia. CXR shows lobar consolidation. PaO₂ remains low despite high-flow O₂. Task: Choose the dominant mechanism and the best immediate nursing focus.

middle_options: Escalate respiratory support (oxygen/ventilation as needed) and treat underlying infection; monitor for fatigue
Encourage paper-bag rebreathing
Restrict oxygen to avoid suppressing drive
Provide diuretics only and discharge if improved

answer.middle: Escalate respiratory support (oxygen/ventilation as needed) and treat underlying infection; monitor for fatigue

rationale: Consolidation → shunt physiology; oxygen response limited → support ventilation/oxygenation and treat cause.

stem: A patient has pleuritic chest pain localized to the lateral chest wall. The most likely sensory nerve carrying this pain is:
rationale: Costal (parietal) pleura is innervated by intercostal nerves → sharp localized pain.

stem: During an emergency airway, a cricothyrotomy is performed. The membrane pierced is between:
choices: Cricoid cartilage and first tracheal ring
rationale: Cricothyroid membrane lies between thyroid and cricoid cartilages.

stem: To avoid damaging the intercostal neurovascular bundle, a needle or chest tube should be inserted:
choices: Through the center of an intercostal space
rationale: Intercostal VAN runs along inferior border of rib → insert just superior to the rib below.

stem: A patient with chronic smoking has destruction of alveolar septa. The primary anatomic change causing decreased gas exchange is loss of:
choices: Alveolar surface area and capillary bed
answer: Alveolar surface area and capillary bed
rationale: Emphysema destroys alveolar walls → reduced surface area + capillary network.

stem: A patient has chronic aspiration while upright. The most common lobe involved is:
choices: Left lower lobe posterior segment only
Right upper lobe posterior segment only
rationale: Upright aspiration tends toward lower lobes, often right.

stem: A child aspirates a peanut that lodges in the right bronchial tree. The most likely specific location is:
rationale: Foreign bodies most commonly lodge in the right main bronchus.

stem: A patient has a mass compressing the left recurrent laryngeal nerve. The most likely neighboring structure involved is:
rationale: Left RLN loops under aortic arch near ligamentum arteriosum.

stem: A patient has sudden pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, and hypoxemia. Oxygen improves saturation but dyspnea persists; D-dimer elevated. This physiology is most consistent with:
rationale: PE causes ventilated but underperfused alveoli → dead space.

stem: A patient has chronic hypoventilation from opioid overdose. The primary ABG change is:
rationale: Hypoventilation → hypercapnic respiratory acidosis.

stem: A patient undergoes segmentectomy. The anatomic feature enabling segmental resection is that pulmonary veins run:
choices: Centrally within each segment with arteries
rationale: Veins define intersegmental boundaries.

stem: A patient has a penetrating injury that causes air to accumulate in the pleural space, collapsing the lung. The layer directly adherent to the lung surface is the:
rationale: Visceral pleura covers the lung; parietal pleura lines chest wall.

stem: A patient’s hoarseness develops after thyroid surgery. The most likely nerve injured that impacts vocal cord movement is:
rationale: Recurrent laryngeal nerve (CN X) innervates intrinsic laryngeal muscles.

stem: Which features distinguish the left lung from the right? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Has an oblique fissure only (no horizontal fissure)
answer: Has an oblique fissure only (no horizontal fissure)
rationale: Left lung: 2 lobes, cardiac notch, lingula, only oblique fissure.

stem: The macula densa senses:
choices: Tubular NaCl delivery in distal tubule
answer: Tubular NaCl delivery in distal tubule
rationale: Macula densa senses NaCl in distal tubular fluid.

stem: A patient has painless gross hematuria and clots. This suggests bleeding source is most likely:
rationale: Clots and painless gross hematuria suggest non-glomerular source (urothelial malignancy until proven otherwise).

stem: A patient has metabolic acidosis with normal anion gap and diarrhea. The main mechanism is:
choices: Loss of bicarbonate from GI tract
answer: Loss of bicarbonate from GI tract
rationale: Diarrhea causes HCO₃⁻ loss → NAGMA.

stem: A patient with nephrotic syndrome is edematous. The hallmark urine finding is:
rationale: Nephrotic syndrome = proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia.

stem: A stone is lodged at the ureteropelvic junction (UPJ). Where is pain most likely felt initially?
rationale: UPJ stones cause flank pain.

stem: Scenario: A patient with severe dehydration has rising creatinine. Urine is concentrated with low urine sodium. Task: Choose the AKI type and the priority intervention focus.

middle_options: Restore perfusion/volume per protocol; monitor urine output and electrolytes
Immediate dialysis without assessment
Restrict fluids strictly
Treat with anticoagulation

answer.middle: Restore perfusion/volume per protocol; monitor urine output and electrolytes

rationale: Prerenal AKI improves with restoring effective circulating volume.

stem: Angiotensin II preferentially constricts which arteriole to help maintain GFR?
rationale: Ang II preferentially constricts efferent arteriole → maintains glomerular pressure.

stem: A patient has painless hematuria and weight loss; imaging shows a renal mass. The most likely malignancy is:
rationale: RCC classically presents with hematuria ± weight loss; “only” options are traps.

stem: The thin descending limb of loop of Henle is:
rationale: Descending limb allows water reabsorption.

stem: A patient is given acetazolamide. This diuretic acts primarily at the:
rationale: Carbonic anhydrase inhibition in PCT.

stem: ADH increases water reabsorption mainly by acting on:
rationale: ADH inserts aquaporin-2 in principal cells.

stem: A patient has recurrent calcium oxalate stones. The most common crystal shape is:
rationale: Calcium oxalate = envelope/dumbbell.

stem: A patient has hematuria, hypertension, and periorbital edema after a recent infection. Urinalysis shows RBC casts. Most likely syndrome:
rationale: RBC casts + HTN + hematuria = nephritic (glomerular inflammation).

stem: A patient has sharp flank pain radiating to the groin with hematuria. Most likely diagnosis:
rationale: Classic ureteral stone pain pattern.

stem: Match stone location to pain pattern. Which are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Mid-ureter → pain radiating to lower abdomen
answer: Mid-ureter → pain radiating to lower abdomen
rationale: UVJ irritates bladder → suprapubic symptoms; shoulder pain is diaphragmatic (phrenic).

stem: Which findings are typical of renal colic from ureteral stone? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Patient often restless, unable to get comfortable
CVA tenderness with high fever is required
answer: Patient often restless, unable to get comfortable
rationale: Fever suggests infection/pyelonephritis; not required for stones.

stem: A patient has hydronephrosis and a distended bladder. After Foley placement, urine output increases dramatically. Most likely diagnosis:
rationale: Bladder outlet obstruction relieved by catheterization.

stem: The arteriole that directly regulates glomerular filtration pressure by entering the glomerulus is the:
rationale: Afferent arteriole delivers blood into glomerulus and strongly influences GFR.

stem: Which findings support prerenal azotemia? (Select all that apply.)
choices: FeNa typically low (<1%) unless on diuretics
answer: FeNa typically low (<1%) unless on diuretics
rationale: Muddy brown casts suggest ATN (intrinsic).

stem: A patient becomes hypotensive from hemorrhage and develops AKI. The earliest injury mechanism is most consistent with:
choices: Prerenal azotemia from decreased renal perfusion
answer: Prerenal azotemia from decreased renal perfusion
rationale: Low effective circulating volume → ↓ renal perfusion → prerenal azotemia.

stem: Which conditions can cause type 4 RTA? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Hyperaldosteronism causes hypokalemic alkalosis, not type 4 RTA.

stem: A patient has persistent vomiting and becomes alkalotic. The kidney response (if volume is restored) is to:
rationale: With adequate volume/chloride, kidneys can excrete HCO₃⁻ to correct alkalosis.

stem: A patient has spinal cord injury above S2–S4. After spinal shock resolves, the bladder pattern is most likely:
choices: Areflexic bladder with overflow incontinence permanently
Spastic/reflex bladder with urgency and poor voluntary control
Complete inability to store urine (always continuous leakage)
answer: Spastic/reflex bladder with urgency and poor voluntary control
rationale: UMN lesion → reflex voiding with loss of cortical inhibition.

stem: Scenario: A patient with heart failure is started on an NSAID and becomes oliguric. Creatinine rises. Task: Choose the mechanism and the priority nursing direction.

left_options: NSAID blocks prostaglandins → afferent arteriole constriction → ↓GFR
middle_options: Escalate concern for hemodynamic AKI, stop offending agent per orders, monitor I&O/electrolytes/renal function
Increase NSAID dose to improve renal perfusion
Restrict monitoring because AKI is always transient
Encourage dehydration to reduce edema

answer.left: NSAID blocks prostaglandins → afferent arteriole constriction → ↓GFR
answer.middle: Escalate concern for hemodynamic AKI, stop offending agent per orders, monitor I&O/electrolytes/renal function

rationale: NSAIDs remove afferent vasodilation support—especially risky in low effective arterial volume states.

stem: Which statements about nephron physiology are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Thick ascending limb reabsorbs Na-K-2Cl and is impermeable to water
Loop diuretics act on thick ascending limb
ADH increases water permeability in collecting duct
Aldosterone increases Na reabsorption and K secretion in collecting duct
answer: Thick ascending limb reabsorbs Na-K-2Cl and is impermeable to water
Loop diuretics act on thick ascending limb
ADH increases water permeability in collecting duct
Aldosterone increases Na reabsorption and K secretion in collecting duct
rationale: PCT is highly water-permeable.

stem: The countercurrent multiplier establishes a medullary osmotic gradient primarily via:
choices: Active NaCl transport in TAL with water-impermeability
Urea secretion in proximal tubule only
answer: Active NaCl transport in TAL with water-impermeability
rationale: TAL pumps salt out without water → builds gradient.

stem: NSAIDs can precipitate AKI in volume-depleted states because they:
rationale: Prostaglandins dilate afferent arteriole; NSAIDs remove that support.

stem: A patient has nephrolithiasis. Which stone type is associated with hexagonal crystals?
rationale: Cystine stones → hexagonal crystals.

stem: A stone is obstructing the ureterovesical junction (UVJ). Most likely symptom pattern:
choices: Flank pain only without urinary symptoms
rationale: UVJ irritation causes bladder symptoms.

stem: Which changes contribute to contraction alkalosis? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Increased H+ secretion in collecting duct
Decreased sympathetic tone as primary driver
answer: Increased H+ secretion in collecting duct
rationale: Volume depletion increases sympathetic tone and RAAS, not decreases.

stem: Scenario: A diabetic patient has metabolic acidosis and hyperkalemia. Labs suggest low aldosterone activity. Task: Choose the RTA type and the key clinical priority.

middle_options: Address hyperkalemia risk (cardiac monitoring, treatment per protocol) and correct acidosis cause
Encourage high-potassium diet
Ignore potassium if pH is low
Give loop diuretics to worsen K loss without monitoring

answer.middle: Address hyperkalemia risk (cardiac monitoring, treatment per protocol) and correct acidosis cause

rationale: Type 4 RTA features hyperkalemia—immediate risk is dysrhythmia.

stem: Which findings are consistent with ATN? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: BUN:Cr is often closer to normal (not very high like prerenal).

stem: Bladder contraction (detrusor) is primarily mediated by:
rationale: Parasympathetic (S2–S4) stimulates detrusor contraction.

stem: Which findings are associated with renal cell carcinoma? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Flank mass/pain (triad is uncommon but classic)
answer: Flank mass/pain (triad is uncommon but classic)
rationale: Fever can occur but not always.

stem: A patient has generalized edema, heavy proteinuria, hyperlipidemia, and oval fat bodies. Diagnosis:
rationale: Nephrotic = heavy proteinuria + edema + hyperlipidemia; oval fat bodies.

stem: Scenario: A patient presents with fever, rigors, flank pain, hypotension, and CT shows an obstructing ureteral stone with hydronephrosis. Task: Choose the diagnosis and priority action.

middle_options: Sepsis resuscitation + antibiotics per protocol and urgent decompression escalation (stent/nephrostomy)
Send home with oral analgesics only
Delay care until fever resolves spontaneously
Give diuretics to “flush” the stone without monitoring

answer.middle: Sepsis resuscitation + antibiotics per protocol and urgent decompression escalation (stent/nephrostomy)

rationale: Infected obstruction is a true emergency—requires antibiotics + decompression.

stem: Scenario: A patient on NSAIDs develops AKI with fever and rash; UA shows eosinophils. Task: Choose the AKI category and the most appropriate nursing direction.

middle_options: Stop offending agent per orders, monitor renal function/electrolytes, escalate evaluation for AIN
Encourage continued NSAID use for inflammation
Give loop diuretics to worsen volume loss without monitoring
Delay care until creatinine doubles again

answer.middle: Stop offending agent per orders, monitor renal function/electrolytes, escalate evaluation for AIN

rationale: AIN is intrinsic AKI; removing trigger and monitoring are essential.

stem: A patient has anuria and bilateral hydronephrosis on ultrasound. Most likely category:
rationale: Bilateral hydronephrosis suggests obstruction.

stem: ACE inhibitors can precipitate AKI in bilateral renal artery stenosis because they:
choices: Dilate efferent arteriole, dropping glomerular pressure
answer: Dilate efferent arteriole, dropping glomerular pressure
rationale: ACEi remove Ang II efferent constriction → GFR drops in RAS.

stem: Which causes can produce postrenal AKI? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Renal artery stenosis is prerenal.

stem: A patient has urinary retention with overflow incontinence after spinal cord injury. The lesion is most consistent with:
choices: UMN lesion above sacral micturition center
LMN lesion involving sacral segments/cauda equina
answer: LMN lesion involving sacral segments/cauda equina
rationale: LMN/sacral involvement → areflexic bladder → retention + overflow.

stem: An acid–base disorder shows: pH 7.29, PaCO₂ 29, HCO₃⁻ 13. Primary disorder:
rationale: Low pH + low HCO₃⁻ → metabolic acidosis.

stem: The nephron segment primarily responsible for water reabsorption independent of ADH is:
rationale: PCT reabsorbs most filtered water and solutes isosmotically.

stem: A patient has metabolic acidosis and hyperkalemia with impaired distal acidification. Most consistent with:
rationale: Type 4 RTA → hypoaldosteronism → hyperkalemia + metabolic acidosis.

stem: A patient has fever, flank pain, and CVA tenderness. UA shows WBC casts. Most likely diagnosis:
rationale: WBC casts indicate upper tract inflammation (pyelonephritis).

stem: A patient has AKI after starting an aminoglycoside. Urine shows muddy brown granular casts. Most likely lesion:
rationale: Aminoglycosides injure proximal tubules → ATN.

stem: A thiazide diuretic tends to cause:
rationale: Thiazides increase Ca reabsorption → hypercalcemia tendency.

stem: A patient has muddy brown granular casts and rising creatinine after prolonged hypotension. Most likely diagnosis:
rationale: Ischemia → tubular injury → ATN with granular casts.

stem: A patient is on a loop diuretic and develops hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis. The key mechanism includes:
choices: Increased distal Na delivery → increased K/H secretion
answer: Increased distal Na delivery → increased K/H secretion
rationale: More Na to distal nephron + RAAS activation → K/H loss → alkalosis.

stem: Which findings support cauda equina / LMN bladder dysfunction? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Spasticity/hyperreflexia suggest UMN.

stem: During a bimanual exam, the clinician palpates the cervix. The cervix is the inferior portion of the:
rationale: The cervix is the lower uterine segment projecting into the vagina.

stem: A postpartum patient has heavy bleeding after uterine atony. Which uterine layer is primarily responsible for powerful contractions that compress vessels?
rationale: Myometrium is the smooth muscle layer that contracts to compress spiral arteries.

stem: A nurse is teaching about an IUD insertion. The uterine cavity is normally lined by which layer?
rationale: Endometrium is the mucosal lining that undergoes cyclical changes.

stem: During laparoscopic salpingectomy, which structure runs in the superior margin of the broad ligament within the mesosalpinx?
rationale: The uterine tube is suspended by the mesosalpinx (part of broad ligament).

stem: A patient has pelvic surgery; the ureter is at risk near the uterine artery. The classic relationship is:
choices: Ureter crosses over the uterine artery ('water over the bridge')
Uterine artery crosses over the ureter ('water under the bridge')
Ureter travels within the round ligament
Ureter enters the uterus through the cervix
answer: Uterine artery crosses over the ureter ('water under the bridge')
rationale: Ureter passes under uterine artery near cervix: 'water (urine) under the bridge'.

stem: During hysterectomy, ligation too lateral risks injuring which structure as it courses along the lateral pelvic wall?
rationale: Ureter descends on lateral pelvic wall and enters bladder near cervix; vulnerable during uterine artery ligation.

stem: A patient with endometriosis has pain referred to the shoulder from diaphragmatic irritation. This referral is carried primarily by:
rationale: Diaphragm irritation refers to shoulder via phrenic (C3–C5).

stem: In males, the structure analogous to the female round ligament of the uterus is the:
rationale: Both derive from gubernaculum; female round ligament/ovarian ligament are remnants.

stem: A testicular torsion compromises blood flow in vessels traveling within which structure?
rationale: Testicular vessels and vas deferens are contained in the spermatic cord.

stem: A varicocele is dilation of pampiniform plexus. The pampiniform plexus drains primarily into the:
rationale: Pampiniform plexus forms testicular vein; left drains to left renal vein; right to IVC.

stem: A patient has painless gross hematuria after pelvic irradiation. Which structure lies immediately anterior to the vagina and is most susceptible to fistula formation?
rationale: Bladder sits anterior to vagina; radiation can predispose to vesicovaginal fistula.

stem: During childbirth, a tear extends into the perineal body. The perineal body is most critical because it provides attachment for:
choices: Multiple perineal muscles supporting pelvic floor
answer: Multiple perineal muscles supporting pelvic floor
rationale: Perineal body anchors bulbospongiosus, EAS, levator ani fibers, etc; integrity supports pelvic floor.

stem: A nurse inserts a catheter in a male patient. The narrowest portion of the male urethra is the:
rationale: Membranous urethra (through urogenital diaphragm/external sphincter) is least distensible and narrow.

stem: A patient undergoes prostatectomy. The nerve plexus most responsible for erectile function and at risk during surgery is the:
rationale: Cavernous nerves from pelvic (inferior hypogastric) plexus mediate erection (parasympathetic S2–S4).

stem: Epididymitis pain can be referred to the lower abdomen via shared spinal segments of the testis, which are primarily:
rationale: Testicular visceral afferents travel with sympathetics to T10–T12.

stem: A woman has pelvic pain worse with hip extension; psoas sign is positive. Which structure can irritate psoas and produce this finding?
rationale: Inflamed appendix can irritate psoas muscle causing pain with hip extension.

stem: In females, the most common site of ectopic pregnancy is the:
rationale: Ampulla is the most common site where fertilization occurs and ectopic implantation frequently happens.

stem: The artery most directly supplying the uterus is the:
rationale: Uterine artery (from internal iliac) supplies uterus; ovarian artery supplies ovary.

stem: The ovarian artery originates most commonly from the:
rationale: Ovarian (gonadal) arteries arise from abdominal aorta.

stem: During childbirth, stretching of the pelvic floor places which nerve at risk for compression leading to postpartum urinary retention?
rationale: Pudendal nerve (S2–S4) supplies external urethral sphincter/perineum; stretching can cause neuropraxia.

stem: A male patient has a hydrocele. The hydrocele is fluid accumulation within the:
rationale: Tunica vaginalis is a peritoneal sac around testis; fluid accumulation causes hydrocele.

stem: The structure that traverses the inguinal canal in females is the:
rationale: Round ligament passes through inguinal canal to labia majora.

stem: A nurse suspects an injury to the external anal sphincter after delivery. The external anal sphincter is primarily innervated by:
rationale: Pudendal nerve supplies external anal sphincter; injury can cause fecal incontinence.

stem: In males, which structure lies posterior to the urinary bladder and can be palpated on rectal exam?
rationale: Seminal vesicles sit posterior to bladder, superior to prostate; palpable in some conditions.

stem: The ductus deferens joins the seminal vesicle duct to form the:
rationale: Ductus deferens + seminal vesicle duct → ejaculatory duct entering prostatic urethra.

stem: A patient with pelvic inflammatory disease has adnexal tenderness. 'Adnexa' most specifically refers to:
rationale: Adnexa includes ovaries, tubes, and supporting tissues.

stem: Which ligament anchors the ovary to the lateral pelvic wall and carries the ovarian vessels?
rationale: Suspensory ligament contains ovarian artery/vein as they enter pelvis.

stem: During a pelvic lymph node dissection for cervical cancer, the first nodes commonly involved are along the:
rationale: Cervix drains to internal iliac and obturator nodes; important for staging.

stem: The lymphatic drainage of the testes is primarily to the:
rationale: Testes originate high and drain to para-aortic nodes; scrotum drains to superficial inguinal.

stem: The lymphatic drainage of the scrotal skin is primarily to the:
rationale: Scrotal skin drains to superficial inguinal nodes.

stem: A vaginal laceration with brisk bleeding risks injury to branches of which artery that supplies the perineum?
rationale: Internal pudendal supplies perineum and external genitalia.

stem: The clitoris is homologous to which male structure?
rationale: Clitoris (glans) is homologous to glans penis; both from genital tubercle.

stem: A postpartum hemorrhage persists despite uterine massage. Suspected retained products prevent full contraction of which uterine region that is most muscular and contractile?
rationale: Fundal myometrium provides strongest contraction to clamp vessels; retained tissue impairs contraction.

stem: A patient reports dyspareunia and deep pelvic pain. Which structure is directly posterior to the vagina and can be involved in a rectovaginal exam?
rationale: Rectum lies posterior to vagina; rectovaginal exam assesses posterior pelvis.

stem: A hysterectomy requires cutting the uterosacral ligaments. These ligaments primarily attach the cervix/upper vagina to the:
rationale: Uterosacral ligaments run from cervix/upper vagina to sacrum, helping support the uterus.

stem: Which structures are derived from the gubernaculum in females? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Gubernaculum becomes ovarian ligament and round ligament; suspensory ligament is a peritoneal fold carrying vessels.

stem: Which findings support injury to the pudendal nerve after delivery? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Urinary retention with weak voluntary sphincter control
answer: Urinary retention with weak voluntary sphincter control
rationale: Pudendal (S2–S4) supplies perineal sensation and external urethral/anal sphincters.

stem: Which structures pass through the inguinal canal in a typical adult male? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Inguinal canal transmits spermatic cord and ilioinguinal nerve; femoral nerve/ureter do not traverse it.

stem: Which structures are located within the spermatic cord? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Spermatic cord contains vas deferens, testicular vessels, pampiniform plexus, nerves/lymphatics.

stem: Which statements about lymphatic drainage are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Scrotum drains to superficial inguinal nodes
Vulva drains to superficial inguinal nodes (many regions)
Cervix drains primarily to axillary nodes
answer: Scrotum drains to superficial inguinal nodes
Vulva drains to superficial inguinal nodes (many regions)
rationale: Gonads drain to para-aortic; external genital skin to inguinal; cervix to pelvic nodes.

stem: Which neurovascular structures are most at risk during ligation of the uterine artery near the cervix? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Ureter runs under uterine artery; accompanying veins are nearby; obturator nerve is more lateral in obturator canal region.

stem: Which structures contribute to the perineal body and pelvic floor support? (Select all that apply.)
rationale: Perineal body anchors multiple perineal muscles and supports pelvic floor.

stem: Which are true regarding the uterine tube? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Ampulla is the most common site of fertilization
Ciliated epithelium aids transport toward uterus
Uterine tube drains to superficial inguinal nodes
answer: Ampulla is the most common site of fertilization
Ciliated epithelium aids transport toward uterus
rationale: Ampulla is widest; lymph drainage follows gonadal pattern (para-aortic), not superficial inguinal.

stem: Which features are consistent with a vesicovaginal fistula? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Continuous leakage of urine through vagina
Urine leakage that stops with coughing
answer: Continuous leakage of urine through vagina
rationale: Bladder-vaginal communication causes continuous leakage and irritation; cough stress incontinence is different.

stem: Which structures are directly involved in erection physiology in males? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Parasympathetic fibers from S2–S4 via pelvic plexus
answer: Parasympathetic fibers from S2–S4 via pelvic plexus
rationale: Erection is parasympathetic (S2–S4) with cavernous nerves; arterial inflow via pudendal branches.

stem: Scenario: During total abdominal hysterectomy, brisk bleeding occurs near the cervix. The surgeon is preparing to ligate the uterine artery. Task: Choose the structure most at risk and the best immediate safety action.

middle_options: Identify ureter course and ligate uterine artery close to uterus; avoid lateral clamp placement
Clamp widely lateral to cervix to capture all tissue quickly
Ignore ureter; it is far from uterine artery
Ligate ovarian vessels only

answer.middle: Identify ureter course and ligate uterine artery close to uterus; avoid lateral clamp placement

rationale: Ureter passes under uterine artery near cervix; safe ligation is close to uterus with ureter identification.

stem: Scenario: Post-delivery patient has severe perineal pain, loss of perineal sensation, and fecal incontinence. Task: Choose the most likely injured nerve and the best targeted assessment focus.

middle_options: Assess anal wink/external anal sphincter tone and perineal sensation; escalate for obstetric evaluation
Assess dorsiflexion strength only
Assess shoulder abduction strength
Assess gag reflex only

answer.middle: Assess anal wink/external anal sphincter tone and perineal sensation; escalate for obstetric evaluation

rationale: Pudendal (S2–S4) supplies perineal sensation and external sphincters; deficits require prompt evaluation.

stem: Scenario: Male patient presents with acute scrotal pain and high-riding testis. Task: Choose the diagnosis and the immediate nursing priority action.

middle_options: Immediate escalation for emergent urologic evaluation; keep NPO and prepare for rapid intervention
Apply heat and schedule outpatient follow-up
Provide scrotal support only and discharge
Delay evaluation until ultrasound next day

answer.middle: Immediate escalation for emergent urologic evaluation; keep NPO and prepare for rapid intervention

rationale: Torsion compromises testicular blood flow; time-sensitive emergency requiring immediate escalation.

stem: Scenario: Patient has continuous watery leakage from the vagina after pelvic surgery. Task: Choose the most likely fistula and the best confirming bedside clue.

middle_options: Leakage increases with bladder filling and may show urine odor; dye test may demonstrate bladder-to-vagina communication
Passage of stool through vagina is the hallmark
Pain only during menses is the hallmark
Bleeding from nose confirms diagnosis

answer.middle: Leakage increases with bladder filling and may show urine odor; dye test may demonstrate bladder-to-vagina communication

rationale: Bladder-vagina communication causes continuous urine leakage; dye testing can help localize.

stem: Scenario: During pelvic surgery, the surgeon must avoid the obturator nerve. Task: Choose the nerve injury deficit and the best focused assessment.

left_options: Loss of thigh adduction strength and medial thigh sensation
Loss of knee extension and patellar reflex
Foot drop with lateral leg numbness
middle_options: Assess ability to adduct thigh against resistance and check medial thigh sensation
Assess patellar reflex only
Assess wrist extension
Assess facial symmetry

answer.left: Loss of thigh adduction strength and medial thigh sensation
answer.middle: Assess ability to adduct thigh against resistance and check medial thigh sensation

rationale: Obturator nerve supplies adductors and medial thigh sensation; injury causes adduction weakness and sensory loss.
